title: Visual system

infobox.value: is the physiological basis of [visual perception](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception") (the ability to [detect and process](/wiki/Perception "Perception") [light](/wiki/Light "Light")). The system detects, [transduces](/wiki/Phototransduction "Phototransduction") and interprets information concerning [light](/wiki/Light "Light") within the [visible range](/wiki/Visible_range "Visible range") to construct an [image](/wiki/Imaging "Imaging") and build a [mental model](/wiki/Mental_model "Mental model") of the surrounding environment. The visual system is associated with the [eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye") and functionally divided into the [optical](/wiki/Optics "Optics") system (including [cornea](/wiki/Cornea "Cornea") and [lens](/wiki/Crystalline_lens "Crystalline lens")) and the [neural](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") system (including the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") and [visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Body parts responsible for vision

This article is about the physiological components involved in vision. For the ability to interpret the surrounding environment, see [Visual perception](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception").

"Visual sensor" redirects here. For electronic visual sensors, see [Visual sensor network](/wiki/Visual_sensor_network "Visual sensor network").

"Visual" redirects here. For the album, see [*Visual* (album)](/wiki/Tab%C3%BA_Tek#Discography "Tabú Tek").

The **visual system** is the physiological basis of [visual perception](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception") (the ability to [detect and process](/wiki/Perception "Perception") [light](/wiki/Light "Light")). The system detects, [transduces](/wiki/Phototransduction "Phototransduction") and interprets information concerning [light](/wiki/Light "Light") within the [visible range](/wiki/Visible_range "Visible range") to construct an [image](/wiki/Imaging "Imaging") and build a [mental model](/wiki/Mental_model "Mental model") of the surrounding environment. The visual system is associated with the [eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye") and functionally divided into the [optical](/wiki/Optics "Optics") system (including [cornea](/wiki/Cornea "Cornea") and [lens](/wiki/Crystalline_lens "Crystalline lens")) and the [neural](/wiki/Nervous_system "Nervous system") system (including the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") and [visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex")).

The visual system performs a number of complex tasks based on the *image forming* functionality of the eye, including the formation of monocular images, the neural mechanisms underlying [stereopsis](/wiki/Stereopsis "Stereopsis") and assessment of distances to ([depth perception](/wiki/Depth_perception "Depth perception")) and between objects, [motion perception](/wiki/Motion_perception "Motion perception"), [pattern recognition](/wiki/Pattern_recognition "Pattern recognition"), accurate [motor coordination](/wiki/Motor_coordination "Motor coordination") under visual guidance, and [colour vision](/wiki/Colour_vision "Colour vision"). Together, these facilitate higher order tasks, such as [object identification](/wiki/Object_recognition_(cognitive_science) "Object recognition (cognitive science)"). The [neuropsychological](/wiki/Neuropsychological "Neuropsychological") side of visual information processing is known as [visual perception](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception"), an abnormality of which is called [visual impairment](/wiki/Visual_impairment "Visual impairment"), and a complete absence of which is called [blindness](/wiki/Blindness "Blindness"). The visual system also has several non-image forming visual functions, independent of visual perception, including the [pupillary light reflex](/wiki/Pupillary_light_reflex "Pupillary light reflex") and circadian [photoentrainment](/wiki/Entrainment_(chronobiology) "Entrainment (chronobiology)").

This article describes the human visual system, which is representative of [mammalian vision](/wiki/Mammalian_vision "Mammalian vision"), and to a lesser extent the [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") visual system.

## System overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: System overview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Comprehensive_List_of_Relevant_Pathways_for_the_Visual_System.png/250px-Comprehensive_List_of_Relevant_Pathways_for_the_Visual_System.png)](/wiki/File:Comprehensive_List_of_Relevant_Pathways_for_the_Visual_System.png)

This diagram linearly (unless otherwise mentioned) tracks the projections of all known structures that allow for vision to their relevant endpoints in the human brain. Click to enlarge the image.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/ERP_-_optic_cabling.jpg/330px-ERP_-_optic_cabling.jpg)](/wiki/File:ERP_-_optic_cabling.jpg)

Representation of optic pathways from each of the 4 quadrants of view for both eyes simultaneously

### Optical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Optical")]

Together, the [cornea](/wiki/Cornea "Cornea") and [lens](/wiki/Lens_(anatomy) "Lens (anatomy)") refract light into a small image and shine it on the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina"). The retina [transduces](/wiki/Visual_phototransduction "Visual phototransduction") this image into electrical pulses using [rods](/wiki/Rods_(eye) "Rods (eye)") and [cones](/wiki/Cones_(eye) "Cones (eye)"). The [optic nerve](/wiki/Optic_nerve "Optic nerve") then carries these pulses through the [optic canal](/wiki/Optic_canal "Optic canal"). Upon reaching the [optic chiasm](/wiki/Optic_chiasm "Optic chiasm") the nerve fibers decussate (left becomes right). The fibers then branch and terminate in three places.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Neural

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Neural")]

Most of the optic nerve fibers end in the [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus") (LGN). Before the LGN forwards the pulses to V1 of the visual cortex (primary) it gauges the range of objects and tags every major object with a velocity tag. These tags predict object movement.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The LGN also sends some fibers to V2 and V3.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

V1 performs edge-detection to understand spatial organization (initially, 40 milliseconds in, focusing on even small spatial and color changes. Then, 100 milliseconds in, upon receiving the translated LGN, V2, and V3 info, also begins focusing on global organization). V1 also creates a bottom-up [saliency map](/wiki/Saliency_map "Saliency map") to guide attention or [gaze shift](/wiki/Gaze_shift "Gaze shift").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

V2 both forwards (direct and via [pulvinar](/wiki/Pulvinar_nuclei "Pulvinar nuclei")) pulses to V1 and receives them. Pulvinar is responsible for [saccade](/wiki/Saccade "Saccade") and visual attention. V2 serves much the same function as V1, however, it also handles [illusory contours](/wiki/Illusory_contours "Illusory contours"), determining depth by comparing left and right pulses (2D images), and foreground distinguishment. V2 connects to V1 - V5.

V3 helps process '[global motion](/wiki/Global_motion "Global motion")' (direction and speed) of objects. V3 connects to V1 (weak), V2, and the [inferior temporal cortex](/wiki/Inferior_temporal_cortex "Inferior temporal cortex").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

V4 recognizes simple shapes, and gets input from V1 (strong), V2, V3, LGN, and pulvinar.[[16]](#cite_note-16) V5's outputs include V4 and its surrounding area, and eye-movement motor cortices ([frontal eye-field](/wiki/Frontal_eye_fields "Frontal eye fields") and [lateral intraparietal area](/wiki/Lateral_intraparietal_cortex "Lateral intraparietal cortex")).

V5's functionality is similar to that of the other V's, however, it integrates local object motion into global motion on a complex level. V6 works in conjunction with V5 on motion analysis. V5 analyzes self-motion, whereas V6 analyzes motion of objects relative to the background. V6's primary input is V1, with V5 additions. V6 houses the [topographical map](/wiki/Topographic_map "Topographic map") for vision. V6 outputs to the region directly around it (V6A). V6A has direct connections to arm-moving cortices, including the [premotor cortex](/wiki/Premotor_cortex "Premotor cortex").[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [inferior temporal gyrus](/wiki/Inferior_temporal_gyrus "Inferior temporal gyrus") recognizes complex shapes, objects, and faces or, in conjunction with the [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus"), creates new [memories](/wiki/Memories "Memories").[[19]](#cite_note-19) The [pretectal area](/wiki/Pretectal_area "Pretectal area") is seven unique [nuclei](/wiki/Nucleus_(neuroanatomy) "Nucleus (neuroanatomy)"). Anterior, posterior and medial pretectal nuclei inhibit pain (indirectly), aid in [REM](/wiki/Rapid_eye_movement_sleep "Rapid eye movement sleep"), and aid the [accommodation reflex](/wiki/Accommodation_reflex "Accommodation reflex"), respectively.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The [Edinger-Westphal nucleus](/wiki/Edinger%E2%80%93Westphal_nucleus "Edinger–Westphal nucleus") moderates [pupil dilation](/wiki/Pupil_dilation "Pupil dilation") and aids (since it provides parasympathetic fibers) in convergence of the eyes and lens adjustment.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Nuclei of the optic tract are involved in smooth pursuit eye movement and the accommodation reflex, as well as REM.

The [suprachiasmatic nucleus](/wiki/Suprachiasmatic_nucleus "Suprachiasmatic nucleus") is the region of the [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus") that halts production of [melatonin](/wiki/Melatonin "Melatonin") (indirectly) at first light.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Schematic_diagram_of_the_human_eye_en.svg/330px-Schematic_diagram_of_the_human_eye_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Schematic_diagram_of_the_human_eye_en.svg)

The [human eye](/wiki/Human_eye "Human eye") (horizontal section)  
*The image projected onto the retina is inverted due to the optics of the eye.*

* The [eye](/wiki/Eye#Eye "Eye"), especially the [retina](#Retina)
* The [optic nerve](#Optic_nerve)
* The [optic chiasma](/wiki/Optic_chiasm#Optic_chiasm "Optic chiasm")
* The [optic tract](#Optic_tract)
* The [lateral geniculate body](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_body#Lateral_geniculate_body "Lateral geniculate body")

These are components of the **visual pathway**, also called the **optic pathway**,[[23]](#cite_note-MSD-23) that can be divided into [anterior and posterior visual pathways](/wiki/Anatomical_terms_of_location#Anterior_and_posterior "Anatomical terms of location"). The anterior visual pathway refers to structures involved in vision before the [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus"). The posterior visual pathway refers to structures after this point.

### Eye

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Eye")]

Main articles: [Eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye") and [Anterior segment of eyeball](/wiki/Anterior_segment_of_eyeball "Anterior segment of eyeball")

Light entering the eye is [refracted](/wiki/Refracted "Refracted") as it passes through the [cornea](/wiki/Cornea "Cornea"). It then passes through the [pupil](/wiki/Pupil "Pupil") (controlled by the [iris](/wiki/Iris_(anatomy) "Iris (anatomy)")) and is further refracted by the [lens](/wiki/Lens_(vision) "Lens (vision)"). The cornea and lens act together as a compound lens to project an inverted image onto the retina.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Cajal_Retina.jpg/250px-Cajal_Retina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cajal_Retina.jpg)

[S. Ramón y Cajal](/wiki/S._Ram%C3%B3n_y_Cajal "S. Ramón y Cajal"), *Structure of the [Mammalian](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") Retina, 1900*

#### Retina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Retina")]

Main article: [Retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina")

The retina consists of many [photoreceptor cells](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") which contain particular [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") [molecules](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") called [opsins](/wiki/Opsin "Opsin"). In humans, two types of opsins are involved in conscious vision: [rod opsins](/wiki/Rod_cell "Rod cell") and [cone opsins](/wiki/Cone_cell "Cone cell"). (A third type, [melanopsin](/wiki/Melanopsin "Melanopsin") in some [retinal ganglion cells](/wiki/Retinal_ganglion_cell "Retinal ganglion cell") (RGC), part of the [body clock](/wiki/Body_clock "Body clock") mechanism, is probably not involved in conscious vision, as these RGC do not project to the [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus") but to the [pretectal olivary nucleus](/wiki/Pretectal_area "Pretectal area").[[24]](#cite_note-24)) An opsin absorbs a [photon](/wiki/Photon "Photon") (a particle of light) and transmits a signal to the [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") through a [signal transduction pathway](/wiki/Signal_transduction_pathway "Signal transduction pathway"), resulting in hyper-polarization of the photoreceptor.

Rods and cones differ in function. Rods are found primarily in the periphery of the retina and are used to see at low levels of light. Each human eye contains 120 million rods. Cones are found primarily in the center (or [fovea](/wiki/Fovea_centralis "Fovea centralis")) of the retina.[[25]](#cite_note-HyperPhysics-25) There are three types of cones that differ in the [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelengths "Wavelengths") of light they absorb; they are usually called short or blue, middle or green, and long or red. Cones mediate day vision and can distinguish [color](/wiki/Color "Color") and other features of the visual world at medium and high light levels. Cones are larger and much less numerous than rods (there are 6-7 million of them in each human eye).[[25]](#cite_note-HyperPhysics-25)

In the retina, the photoreceptors [synapse](/wiki/Synapse "Synapse") directly onto [bipolar cells](/wiki/Bipolar_cell_of_the_retina "Bipolar cell of the retina"), which in turn synapse onto [ganglion cells](/wiki/Retinal_ganglion_cell "Retinal ganglion cell") of the outermost layer, which then conduct [action potentials](/wiki/Action_potentials "Action potentials") to the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain"). A significant amount of [visual processing](/wiki/Visual_processing "Visual processing") arises from the patterns of communication between [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") in the retina. About 130 million photo-receptors absorb light, yet roughly 1.2 million [axons](/wiki/Axons "Axons") of ganglion cells transmit information from the retina to the brain. The processing in the retina includes the formation of center-surround [receptive fields](/wiki/Receptive_fields "Receptive fields") of bipolar and ganglion cells in the retina, as well as convergence and divergence from photoreceptor to bipolar cell. In addition, other neurons in the retina, particularly [horizontal](/wiki/Horizontal_cell "Horizontal cell") and [amacrine cells](/wiki/Amacrine_cell "Amacrine cell"), transmit information laterally (from a neuron in one layer to an adjacent neuron in the same layer), resulting in more complex receptive fields that can be either indifferent to color and sensitive to [motion](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)") or sensitive to color and indifferent to motion.[[26]](#cite_note-Tov2008-26)

##### Mechanism of generating visual signals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mechanism of generating visual signals")]

The retina adapts to change in light through the use of the rods. In the dark, the [chromophore](/wiki/Chromophore "Chromophore") [retinal](/wiki/Retinal "Retinal") has a bent shape called cis-retinal (referring to a *cis* conformation in one of the double bonds). When light interacts with the retinal, it changes conformation to a straight form called trans-retinal and breaks away from the opsin. This is called bleaching because the purified [rhodopsin](/wiki/Rhodopsin "Rhodopsin") changes from violet to colorless in the light. At baseline in the dark, the rhodopsin absorbs no light and releases [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate"), which inhibits the bipolar cell. This inhibits the release of neurotransmitters from the bipolar cells to the ganglion cell. When there is light present, glutamate secretion ceases, thus no longer inhibiting the bipolar cell from releasing neurotransmitters to the ganglion cell and therefore an image can be detected.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The final result of all this processing is five different populations of ganglion cells that send visual (image-forming and non-image-forming) information to the brain:[[26]](#cite_note-Tov2008-26)

1. M cells, with large center-surround receptive fields that are sensitive to [depth](/wiki/Depth_perception "Depth perception"), indifferent to color, and rapidly adapt to a stimulus;
2. P cells, with smaller center-surround receptive fields that are sensitive to color and [shape](/wiki/Shape "Shape");
3. K cells, with very large center-only receptive fields that are sensitive to color and indifferent to shape or depth;
4. [another population that is intrinsically photosensitive](/wiki/Photosensitive_ganglion_cell "Photosensitive ganglion cell"); and
5. a final population that is used for eye movements.[[26]](#cite_note-Tov2008-26)

A 2006 [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") study calculated the approximate [bandwidth](/wiki/Bandwidth_(computing) "Bandwidth (computing)") of human retinas to be about 8,960 [kilobits](/wiki/Kilobit "Kilobit") per second, whereas [guinea pig](/wiki/Guinea_pig "Guinea pig") retinas transfer at about 875 kilobits.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In 2007 Zaidi and co-researchers on both sides of the Atlantic studying patients without rods and cones, discovered that the novel photoreceptive ganglion cell in humans also has a role in conscious and unconscious visual perception.[[30]](#cite_note-Zaidi,_2007-30) The peak [spectral sensitivity](/wiki/Spectral_sensitivity "Spectral sensitivity") was 481 nm. This shows that there are two pathways for vision in the retina – one based on classic photoreceptors (rods and cones) and the other, newly discovered, based on photo-receptive ganglion cells which act as rudimentary visual brightness detectors.

#### Photochemistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Photochemistry")]

Main article: [Visual cycle](/wiki/Visual_cycle "Visual cycle")

The functioning of a [camera](/wiki/Camera "Camera") is often compared with the workings of the eye, mostly since both focus light from external objects in the [field of view](/wiki/Field_of_view "Field of view") onto a light-sensitive medium. In the case of the camera, this medium is film or an electronic sensor; in the case of the eye, it is an array of visual receptors. With this simple geometrical similarity, based on the laws of optics, the eye functions as a [transducer](/wiki/Transducer "Transducer"), as does a [CCD camera](/wiki/Charge-coupled_device "Charge-coupled device").

In the visual system, **retinal**, technically called *[retinene](/wiki/Retinene "Retinene")*1 or "retinaldehyde", is a light-sensitive molecule found in the rods and cones of the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina"). Retinal is the fundamental structure involved in the transduction of [light](/wiki/Light "Light") into visual signals, i.e. nerve impulses in the ocular system of the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"). In the presence of light, the retinal molecule changes configuration and as a result, a [nerve impulse](/wiki/Nerve_impulse "Nerve impulse") is generated.[[26]](#cite_note-Tov2008-26)

### Optic nerve

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Optic nerve")]

Main article: [Optic nerve](/wiki/Optic_nerve "Optic nerve")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/1543%2CVesalius%27Fabrica%2CVisualSystem%2CV1.jpg/250px-1543%2CVesalius%27Fabrica%2CVisualSystem%2CV1.jpg)](/wiki/File:1543,Vesalius%27Fabrica,VisualSystem,V1.jpg)

Information flow from the [eyes](/wiki/Eye "Eye") (top), crossing at the [optic chiasma](/wiki/Optic_chiasm "Optic chiasm"), joining left and right eye information in the [optic tract](/wiki/Optic_tract "Optic tract"), and layering left and right visual stimuli in the [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus"). [V1](/wiki/Visual_cortex#Primary_visual_cortex_(V1) "Visual cortex") in red at bottom of image. (1543 image from [Andreas Vesalius](/wiki/Andreas_Vesalius "Andreas Vesalius")' *Fabrica*)

The information about the image via the eye is transmitted to the brain along the [optic nerve](/wiki/Optic_nerve "Optic nerve"). Different populations of ganglion cells in the retina send information to the brain through the optic nerve. About 90% of the [axons](/wiki/Axons "Axons") in the optic nerve go to the [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus") in the [thalamus](/wiki/Thalamus "Thalamus"). These axons originate from the M, P, and K ganglion cells in the retina, see above. This [parallel processing](/wiki/Parallel_processing_(psychology) "Parallel processing (psychology)") is important for reconstructing the visual world; each type of information will go through a different route to [perception](/wiki/Perception "Perception"). Another population sends information to the [superior colliculus](/wiki/Superior_colliculus "Superior colliculus") in the [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain"), which assists in controlling eye movements ([saccades](/wiki/Saccades "Saccades"))[[31]](#cite_note-nolte-31) as well as other motor responses.

A final population of [photosensitive ganglion cells](/wiki/Photosensitive_ganglion_cell "Photosensitive ganglion cell"), containing [melanopsin](/wiki/Melanopsin "Melanopsin") for [photosensitivity](/wiki/Photosensitivity "Photosensitivity"), sends information via the [retinohypothalamic tract](/wiki/Retinohypothalamic_tract "Retinohypothalamic tract") to the [pretectum](/wiki/Pretectum "Pretectum") ([pupillary reflex](/wiki/Pupillary_reflex "Pupillary reflex")), to several structures involved in the control of [circadian rhythms](/wiki/Circadian_rhythms "Circadian rhythms") and [sleep](/wiki/Sleep "Sleep") such as the [suprachiasmatic nucleus](/wiki/Suprachiasmatic_nucleus "Suprachiasmatic nucleus") (the biological clock), and to the [ventrolateral preoptic nucleus](/wiki/Ventrolateral_preoptic_nucleus "Ventrolateral preoptic nucleus") (a region involved in [sleep regulation](/wiki/Sleep_regulation "Sleep regulation")).[[32]](#cite_note-32) A recently discovered role for photoreceptive ganglion cells is that they mediate conscious and unconscious vision – acting as rudimentary visual brightness detectors as shown in rodless coneless eyes.[[30]](#cite_note-Zaidi,_2007-30)

### Optic chiasm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Optic chiasm")]

Main article: [Optic chiasm](/wiki/Optic_chiasm "Optic chiasm")

The optic nerves from both eyes meet and cross at the optic chiasm,[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) at the base of the [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus") of the brain. At this point, the information coming from both eyes is combined and then splits according to the [visual field](/wiki/Visual_field "Visual field"). The corresponding halves of the field of view (right and left) are sent to the left and right [halves of the brain](/wiki/Cerebral_hemisphere "Cerebral hemisphere"), respectively, to be processed. That is, the right side of [primary visual cortex](/wiki/Primary_visual_cortex "Primary visual cortex") deals with the left half of the *field of view* from both eyes, and similarly for the left brain.[[31]](#cite_note-nolte-31) A small region in the center of the field of view is processed redundantly by both halves of the brain.

### Optic tract

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Optic tract")]

Main article: [Optic tract](/wiki/Optic_tract "Optic tract")

Information from the right *visual field* (now on the left side of the brain) travels in the left optic tract. Information from the left *visual field* travels in the right optic tract. Each optic tract terminates in the [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus") (LGN) in the thalamus.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus.png/250px-Lateral_geniculate_nucleus.png)](/wiki/File:Lateral_geniculate_nucleus.png)

Six layers in the [LGN](/wiki/Lgn "Lgn")

### Lateral geniculate nucleus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Lateral geniculate nucleus")]

:   Main article: [Lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus")

The **lateral geniculate nucleus** (LGN) is a sensory relay nucleus in the thalamus of the brain. The LGN consists of six layers in [humans](/wiki/Human "Human") and other [primates](/wiki/Primate "Primate") starting from [catarrhines](/wiki/Catarrhini "Catarrhini"), including [cercopithecidae](/wiki/Cercopithecidae "Cercopithecidae") and [apes](/wiki/Ape "Ape"). Layers 1, 4, and 6 correspond to information from the contralateral (crossed) fibers of the nasal retina (temporal visual field); layers 2, 3, and 5 correspond to [information](/wiki/Information "Information") from the ipsilateral (uncrossed) fibers of the temporal retina (nasal visual field).

Layer one contains M cells, which correspond to the M ([magnocellular](/wiki/Magnocellular_cell "Magnocellular cell")) cells of the optic nerve of the opposite eye and are concerned with depth or motion. Layers four and six of the LGN also connect to the opposite eye, but to the P cells (color and edges) of the optic nerve. By contrast, layers two, three and five of the LGN connect to the M cells and P ([parvocellular](/wiki/Parvocellular_cell "Parvocellular cell")) cells of the optic nerve for the same side of the brain as its respective LGN.

Spread out, the six layers of the LGN are the area of a [credit card](/wiki/Credit_card "Credit card") and about three times its thickness. The LGN is rolled up into two [ellipsoids](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid") about the size and shape of two small birds' eggs. In between the six layers are smaller cells that receive information from the K cells (color) in the retina. The neurons of the LGN then relay the visual image to the [primary visual cortex](/wiki/Primary_visual_cortex "Primary visual cortex") (V1) which is located at the back of the brain ([posterior end](/wiki/Posterior_(anatomy) "Posterior (anatomy)")) in the [occipital lobe](/wiki/Occipital_lobe "Occipital lobe") in and close to the [calcarine sulcus](/wiki/Calcarine_sulcus "Calcarine sulcus"). The LGN is not just a simple relay station, but it is also a center for processing; it receives reciprocal input from the [cortical](/wiki/Cortical_layer "Cortical layer") and subcortical layers and [reciprocal innervation](/wiki/Reciprocal_innervation "Reciprocal innervation") from the visual cortex.[[26]](#cite_note-Tov2008-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Lisa_analysis.png/250px-Lisa_analysis.png)](/wiki/File:Lisa_analysis.png)

Scheme of the [optic tract](/wiki/Optic_tract "Optic tract") with image being decomposed on the way, up to simple cortical cells (simplified)

### Optic radiation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Optic radiation")]

Main article: [Optic radiation](/wiki/Optic_radiation "Optic radiation")

The **optic radiations**, one on each side of the brain, carry information from the thalamic [lateral geniculate nucleus](/wiki/Lateral_geniculate_nucleus "Lateral geniculate nucleus") to layer 4 of the [visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex"). The P layer neurons of the LGN relay to V1 layer 4C β. The M layer neurons relay to V1 layer 4C α. The K layer neurons in the LGN relay to large neurons called blobs in layers 2 and 3 of V1.[[26]](#cite_note-Tov2008-26)

There is a direct correspondence from an angular position in the [visual field](/wiki/Visual_field "Visual field") of the eye, all the way through the optic tract to a nerve position in V1 up to V4, i.e. the primary visual areas. After that, the visual pathway is roughly separated into a [ventral and dorsal pathway](/wiki/Two-streams_hypothesis "Two-streams hypothesis").

### Visual cortex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Visual cortex")]

Main article: [Visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Brodmann_areas_17_18_19.png/250px-Brodmann_areas_17_18_19.png)](/wiki/File:Brodmann_areas_17_18_19.png)

[Visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex"):   
V1; V2; V3; V4; V5 (also called MT)

The visual cortex is responsible for processing the visual image. It lies at the rear of the brain (highlighted in the image), above the [cerebellum](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum"). The region that receives information directly from the LGN is called the [primary visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex#Primary_visual_cortex_(V1) "Visual cortex") (also called V1 and striate cortex). It creates a bottom-up saliency map of the visual field to guide attention or eye gaze to salient visual locations.[[35]](#cite_note-:0-35)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Hence selection of visual input information by attention starts at V1[[36]](#cite_note-36) along the visual pathway.

Visual information then flows through a cortical hierarchy. These areas include V2, V3, V4 and area V5/MT. (The exact connectivity depends on the species of the animal.) These secondary visual areas (collectively termed the extrastriate visual cortex) process a wide variety of visual primitives. Neurons in V1 and V2 respond selectively to bars of specific orientations, or combinations of bars. These are believed to support edge and corner detection. Similarly, basic information about color and motion is processed here.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Heider, et al. (2002) found that neurons involving V1, V2, and V3 can detect stereoscopic [illusory contours](/wiki/Illusory_contours "Illusory contours"); they found that stereoscopic stimuli subtending up to 8° can activate these neurons.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/RestingStateModels.jpg/250px-RestingStateModels.jpg)](/wiki/File:RestingStateModels.jpg)

Visual cortex is active even during [resting state fMRI](/wiki/Resting_state_fMRI "Resting state fMRI").

### Visual association cortex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Visual association cortex")]

Main article: [Two-streams hypothesis](/wiki/Two-streams_hypothesis "Two-streams hypothesis")

As visual information passes forward through the visual hierarchy, the complexity of the neural representations increases. Whereas a V1 neuron may respond selectively to a line segment of a particular orientation in a particular [retinotopic](/wiki/Retinotopy "Retinotopy") location, neurons in the lateral occipital complex respond selectively to a complete object (e.g., a figure drawing), and neurons in the visual association cortex may respond selectively to human faces, or to a particular object.

Along with this increasing complexity of neural representation may come a level of specialization of processing into two distinct pathways: the [dorsal stream](/wiki/Dorsal_stream "Dorsal stream") and the [ventral stream](/wiki/Ventral_stream "Ventral stream") (the [Two Streams hypothesis](/wiki/Two_Streams_hypothesis "Two Streams hypothesis"),[[39]](#cite_note-UngerleiderMishkin-39) first proposed by Ungerleider and Mishkin in 1982). The dorsal stream, commonly referred to as the "where" stream, is involved in spatial attention (covert and overt), and communicates with regions that control eye movements and hand movements. More recently, this area has been called the "how" stream to emphasize its role in guiding behaviors to spatial locations. The ventral stream, commonly referred to as the "what" stream, is involved in the recognition, identification and categorization of visual stimuli.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Gray726_intraparietal_sulcus.svg/250px-Gray726_intraparietal_sulcus.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gray726_intraparietal_sulcus.svg)

[Intraparietal sulcus](/wiki/Intraparietal_sulcus "Intraparietal sulcus") (red)

However, there is still much debate about the degree of specialization within these two pathways, since they are in fact heavily interconnected.[[40]](#cite_note-Farivar-40)

[Horace Barlow](/wiki/Horace_Barlow "Horace Barlow") proposed the *[efficient coding hypothesis](/wiki/Efficient_coding_hypothesis "Efficient coding hypothesis")* in 1961 as a theoretical model of [sensory coding](/wiki/Sensory_neuroscience "Sensory neuroscience") in the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain").[[41]](#cite_note-41) Limitations in the applicability of this theory in the [primary visual cortex (V1)](http://www.scholarpedia.org/article/Area_V1) motivated the [V1 Saliency Hypothesis](/wiki/V1_Saliency_Hypothesis "V1 Saliency Hypothesis") that V1 creates a bottom-up saliency map to guide attention exogenously.[[35]](#cite_note-:0-35) With attentional selection as a center stage, vision is seen as composed of encoding, selection, and decoding stages.[[42]](#cite_note-:1-42)

The [default mode network](/wiki/Default_mode_network "Default mode network") is a network of brain regions that are active when an individual is awake and at rest. The visual system's default mode can be monitored during [resting state fMRI](/wiki/Resting_state_fMRI "Resting state fMRI"):
Fox, et al. (2005) found that "[the human brain is intrinsically organized into dynamic, anticorrelated functional networks"](http://www.pnas.org/content/102/27/9673.full),[[43]](#cite_note-43) in which the visual system switches from resting state to attention.

In the [parietal lobe](/wiki/Parietal_lobe "Parietal lobe"), the [lateral](/wiki/Lateral_intraparietal_cortex "Lateral intraparietal cortex") and ventral intraparietal cortex are involved in visual attention and saccadic eye movements. These regions are in the [intraparietal sulcus](/wiki/Intraparietal_sulcus "Intraparietal sulcus") (marked in red in the adjacent image).

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Development")]

### Infancy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Infancy")]

See also: [Infant vision](/wiki/Infant_vision "Infant vision")

Newborn infants have limited [color perception](/wiki/Color_perception "Color perception").[[44]](#cite_note-Lane2012-44) One study found that 74% of newborns can distinguish red, 36% green, 25% yellow, and 14% blue. After one month, performance "improved somewhat."[[45]](#cite_note-AdamsCourage1994-45) Infant's eyes do not have the ability to [accommodate](/wiki/Accommodation_(eye) "Accommodation (eye)"). Pediatricians are able to perform non-verbal testing to assess [visual acuity](/wiki/Visual_acuity "Visual acuity") of a newborn, detect [nearsightedness](/wiki/Nearsightedness "Nearsightedness") and [astigmatism](/wiki/Astigmatism "Astigmatism"), and evaluate the eye teaming and alignment. Visual acuity improves from about 20/400 at birth to approximately 20/25 at 6 months of age. This happens because the nerve cells in the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") and brain that control vision are not fully developed.

### Childhood and adolescence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Childhood and adolescence")]

[Depth perception](/wiki/Depth_perception "Depth perception"), focus, tracking and other aspects of vision continue to develop throughout early and middle childhood. From recent studies in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") there is some evidence that the amount of time school aged children spend outdoors, in natural light, may have some impact on whether they develop [myopia](/wiki/Myopia "Myopia"). The condition tends to get somewhat worse through childhood and adolescence, but stabilizes in adulthood. More prominent myopia (nearsightedness) and astigmatism are thought to be inherited. Children with this condition may need to wear glasses.

### Adulthood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Adulthood")]

Vision is often one of the first senses affected by aging. A number of changes occur with aging:

* Over time, the [lens](/wiki/Lens_(anatomy) "Lens (anatomy)") becomes yellowed and may eventually become brown, a condition known as brunescence or [brunescent](/wiki/Brunescent_cataract "Brunescent cataract") [cataract](/wiki/Cataract "Cataract"). Although many factors contribute to yellowing, lifetime exposure to [ultraviolet light](/wiki/Ultraviolet_light "Ultraviolet light") and [aging](/wiki/Ageing "Ageing") are two main causes.
* The [vitreous humor](/wiki/Vitreous_body "Vitreous body") naturally [degenerates](/wiki/Degenerative_disease "Degenerative disease") over time, causing it to liquefy, contract, and eventually separate from the posterior of the retina. This process leaves deposits of protein structures and other cellular waste within the vitrous humor, visualizing as [eye floaters](/wiki/Floater "Floater").
* [Presbyopia](/wiki/Presbyopia "Presbyopia") develops as the lens gradually loses its natural flexibility, reducing the eye's ability to focus.
* While a healthy adult pupil typically has a size range of 2–8 mm, with age the range gets smaller, trending towards a moderately small diameter.
* [Tear production](/wiki/Tears "Tears") typically declines with age. However, there are a number of age-related conditions that can cause excessive tearing.

## Other functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Other functions")]

### Balance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Balance")]

Along with [proprioception](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception") and [vestibular function](/wiki/Vestibular_function "Vestibular function"), the visual system plays an important role in the ability of an individual to control balance and maintain an upright posture. When these three conditions are isolated and balance is tested, it has been found that vision is the most significant contributor to balance, playing a bigger role than either of the two other intrinsic mechanisms.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The clarity with which an individual can see his environment, as well as the size of the visual field, the susceptibility of the individual to light and glare, and poor depth perception play important roles in providing a feedback loop to the brain on the body's movement through the environment. Anything that affects any of these variables can have a negative effect on balance and maintaining posture.[[47]](#cite_note-47) This effect has been seen in research involving elderly subjects when compared to young controls,[[48]](#cite_note-48) in [glaucoma](/wiki/Glaucoma "Glaucoma") patients compared to age matched controls,[[49]](#cite_note-Shabana,_2005-49) [cataract](/wiki/Cataract "Cataract") patients pre and post surgery,[[50]](#cite_note-Schwartz,_2005-50) and even something as simple as wearing safety goggles.[[51]](#cite_note-51) [Monocular vision](/wiki/Monocular_vision "Monocular vision") (one eyed vision) has also been shown to negatively impact balance, which was seen in the previously referenced cataract and glaucoma studies,[[49]](#cite_note-Shabana,_2005-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Schwartz,_2005-50) as well as in healthy children and adults.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

According to Pollock et al. (2010) [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") is the main cause of specific visual impairment, most frequently visual field loss ([homonymous hemianopia](/wiki/Homonymous_hemianopsia "Homonymous hemianopsia"), a visual field defect). Nevertheless, evidence for the efficacy of cost-effective interventions aimed at these visual field defects is still inconsistent.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Hemianopsia_en.jpg/500px-Hemianopsia_en.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hemianopsia_en.jpg)

**[Visual pathway lesions](/wiki/Visual_pathway_lesions "Visual pathway lesions")**   
 From top to bottom:   
 1. Complete loss of vision, right eye   
 2. [Bitemporal hemianopia](/wiki/Bitemporal_hemianopia "Bitemporal hemianopia")   
 3. [Homonymous hemianopsia](/wiki/Homonymous_hemianopsia "Homonymous hemianopsia")   
 4. [Quadrantanopia](/wiki/Quadrantanopia "Quadrantanopia")   
 5&6. Quadrantanopia with [macular sparing](/wiki/Macular_sparing "Macular sparing")

Proper function of the visual system is required for sensing, processing, and understanding the surrounding environment. Difficulty in sensing, processing and understanding light input has the potential to adversely impact an individual's ability to communicate, learn and effectively complete routine tasks on a daily basis.

In children, early diagnosis and treatment of impaired visual system function is an important factor in ensuring that key social, academic and speech/language developmental milestones are met.

[Cataract](/wiki/Cataract "Cataract") is clouding of the lens, which in turn affects vision. Although it may be accompanied by yellowing, clouding and yellowing can occur separately. This is typically a result of ageing, disease, or drug use.

[Presbyopia](/wiki/Presbyopia "Presbyopia") is a visual condition that causes [farsightedness](/wiki/Far-sightedness "Far-sightedness"). The eye's lens becomes too inflexible to [accommodate](/wiki/Accommodation_(eye) "Accommodation (eye)") to normal reading distance, focus tending to remain fixed at long distance.

[Glaucoma](/wiki/Glaucoma "Glaucoma") is a type of blindness that begins at the edge of the visual field and progresses inward. It may result in [tunnel vision](/wiki/Tunnel_vision "Tunnel vision"). This typically involves the outer layers of the optic nerve, sometimes as a result of buildup of fluid and excessive pressure in the eye.[[54]](#cite_note-Publications-54)

[Scotoma](/wiki/Scotoma "Scotoma") is a type of blindness that produces a small [blind spot](/wiki/Blind_spot_(vision) "Blind spot (vision)") in the visual field typically caused by injury in the primary visual cortex.

[Homonymous hemianopia](/wiki/Homonymous_hemianopia "Homonymous hemianopia") is a type of blindness that destroys one entire side of the visual field typically caused by injury in the primary visual cortex.

[Quadrantanopia](/wiki/Quadrantanopia "Quadrantanopia") is a type of blindness that destroys only a part of the visual field typically caused by partial injury in the primary visual cortex. This is very similar to homonymous hemianopia, but to a lesser degree.

[Prosopagnosia](/wiki/Prosopagnosia "Prosopagnosia"), or face blindness, is a brain disorder that produces an inability to recognize faces. This disorder often arises after damage to the [fusiform face area](/wiki/Fusiform_face_area "Fusiform face area").

[Visual agnosia](/wiki/Visual_agnosia "Visual agnosia"), or visual-form agnosia, is a brain disorder that produces an inability to recognize objects. This disorder often arises after damage to the [ventral stream](/wiki/Ventral_stream "Ventral stream").

## Other animals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other animals")]

See also: [Eye](/wiki/Eye "Eye"), [Vision in birds](/wiki/Vision_in_birds "Vision in birds"), [Parietal eye](/wiki/Parietal_eye "Parietal eye"), [Vision in fish](/wiki/Vision_in_fish "Vision in fish"), [Arthropod visual system](/wiki/Arthropod_visual_system "Arthropod visual system"), and [Cephalopod eye](/wiki/Cephalopod_eye "Cephalopod eye")

Different [species](/wiki/Species "Species") are able to see different parts of the [light spectrum](/wiki/Light_spectrum "Light spectrum"); for example, [bees](/wiki/Bee "Bee") can see into the [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet"),[[55]](#cite_note-55) while [pit vipers](/wiki/Pit_viper "Pit viper") can accurately target prey with their [pit organs](/wiki/Pit_organ "Pit organ"), which are sensitive to infrared radiation.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The [mantis shrimp](/wiki/Mantis_shrimp "Mantis shrimp") possesses arguably the most complex visual system of any species. The eye of the mantis shrimp holds 16 color receptive cones, whereas humans only have three. The variety of cones enables them to perceive an enhanced array of colors as a mechanism for mate selection, avoidance of predators, and detection of prey.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Swordfish also possess an impressive visual system. The eye of a [swordfish](/wiki/Swordfish "Swordfish") can generate [heat](/wiki/Heat "Heat") to better cope with detecting their [prey](/wiki/Prey "Prey") at depths of 2000 feet.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Certain [one-celled](/wiki/One-celled "One-celled") [microorganisms](/wiki/Microorganism "Microorganism"), the [warnowiid](/wiki/Warnowiid "Warnowiid") [dinoflagellates](/wiki/Dinoflagellate "Dinoflagellate") have eye-like [ocelloids](/wiki/Ocelloid "Ocelloid"), with analogous structures for the lens and retina of the multi-cellular eye.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The armored shell of the [chiton](/wiki/Chiton "Chiton") *[Acanthopleura granulata](/wiki/Acanthopleura_granulata "Acanthopleura granulata")* is also covered with hundreds of [aragonite](/wiki/Aragonite "Aragonite") crystalline eyes, named [ocelli](/wiki/Simple_eye_in_invertebrates "Simple eye in invertebrates"), which can form [images](/wiki/Image "Image").[[60]](#cite_note-60)

Many [fan worms](/wiki/Fan_worm "Fan worm"), such as *[Acromegalomma interruptum](/wiki/Acromegalomma_interruptum "Acromegalomma interruptum")* which live in tubes on the sea floor of the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), have evolved compound eyes on their tentacles, which they use to detect encroaching movement. If movement is detected, the fan worms will rapidly withdraw their tentacles. Bok, et al., have discovered opsins and [G proteins](/wiki/G_protein "G protein") in the fan worm's eyes, which were previously only seen in simple [ciliary](/wiki/Cilium "Cilium") photoreceptors in the brains of some [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate"), as opposed to the [rhabdomeric](/wiki/Rhabdomeric "Rhabdomeric") receptors in the eyes of most invertebrates.[[61]](#cite_note-Bok2017-61)

Only [higher primate](/wiki/Higher_primate "Higher primate") [Old World](/wiki/Old_World "Old World") (African) [monkeys](/wiki/Monkey "Monkey") and apes ([macaques](/wiki/Macaque "Macaque"), [apes](/wiki/Ape "Ape"), [orangutans](/wiki/Orangutan "Orangutan")) have the same kind of three-cone [photoreceptor](/wiki/Photoreceptor_cell "Photoreceptor cell") color vision humans have, while lower primate [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") (South American) monkeys ([spider monkeys](/wiki/Spider_monkey "Spider monkey"), [squirrel monkeys](/wiki/Squirrel_monkey "Squirrel monkey"), [cebus monkeys](/wiki/Capuchin_monkey "Capuchin monkey")) have a two-cone photoreceptor kind of color vision.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Biologists have determined that humans have extremely good vision compared to the overwhelming majority of animals, particularly in daylight, surpassed only by a few large species of [predatory birds](/wiki/Bird_of_prey "Bird of prey").[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) Other animals such as [dogs](/wiki/Dog "Dog") are thought to rely more on senses other than vision, which in turn may be better developed than in humans.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Visual_system&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: History")]

In the second half of the 19th century, many motifs of the nervous system were identified such as the neuron doctrine and brain localization, which related to the [neuron](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") being the basic unit of the nervous system and functional localisation in the brain, respectively. These would become tenets of the fledgling [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience") and would support further understanding of the visual system.

The notion that the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex") is divided into functionally distinct cortices now known to be responsible for capacities such as [touch](/wiki/Touch "Touch") ([somatosensory cortex](/wiki/Somatosensory_cortex "Somatosensory cortex")), [movement](/wiki/Motion_(physics) "Motion (physics)") ([motor cortex](/wiki/Motor_cortex "Motor cortex")), and vision ([visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex")), was first proposed by [Franz Joseph Gall](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_Gall "Franz Joseph Gall") in 1810.[[67]](#cite_note-Gross,_1994-67) Evidence for functionally distinct areas of the brain (and, specifically, of the cerebral cortex) mounted throughout the 19th century with discoveries by [Paul Broca](/wiki/Paul_Broca "Paul Broca") of the [language center](/wiki/Language_center "Language center") (1861), and [Gustav Fritsch](/wiki/Gustav_Fritsch "Gustav Fritsch") and [Eduard Hitzig](/wiki/Eduard_Hitzig "Eduard Hitzig") of the motor cortex (1871).[[67]](#cite_note-Gross,_1994-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Schiller,_1986-68) Based on selective damage to parts of the brain and the functional effects of the resulting [lesions](/wiki/Lesion "Lesion"), [David Ferrier](/wiki/David_Ferrier "David Ferrier") proposed that visual function was localized to the [parietal lobe](/wiki/Parietal_lobe "Parietal lobe") of the brain in 1876.[[68]](#cite_note-Schiller,_1986-68) In 1881, [Hermann Munk](/wiki/Hermann_Munk "Hermann Munk") more accurately located vision in the [occipital lobe](/wiki/Occipital_lobe "Occipital lobe"), where the [primary visual cortex](/wiki/Primary_visual_cortex "Primary visual cortex") is now known to be.[[68]](#cite_note-Schiller,_1986-68)

In 2014, a textbook "Understanding vision: theory, models, and data" [[42]](#cite_note-:1-42) illustrates how to link neurobiological data and visual behavior/psychological data through theoretical principles and computational models.
